(静岡県)|森町]][[小国神社]]所有。同社では、[[1572年]]（[[元亀]]3年）、[[武田信玄]]軍と[[織田信長]]・[[徳川家康]]の連合軍が争った[[三方ヶ原の戦い]]の際に、[[徳川家康]]が戦勝祈願として三条宗近の作とされる本太刀を同社に奉納したと伝わっている。 ;太刀銘伝三条小鍛治宗近作 :太刀「[https://www.city.niihama.lg.jp/soshiki/sportsbunka/shiteibunkazai.html 指定・登録文化財]」新居浜市ホームページ。[[愛媛県]][[新居浜市]][[一宮神社 (新居浜市)|一宮神社]]所有。本太刀は、同社の宝物として伝わっている「[http://ikkunomori.com/zinja/ 一宮神社 境内史蹟]」一宮の杜ミュージアムホームページ。新居浜市指定文化財（[[1965年]]（昭和40年）[[6月3日]]指定）。 ;三条宗近銘太刀 :太刀。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]、[[出雲国]][[意宇郡]]宍道（現在の[[島根県]][[松江市]]宍道町）周辺を本拠としていた[[武将]]の[[宍道政慶]]は、当時その一体を支配していた[[毛利氏]]の政策で[[長門国]][[阿武郡]]に転封となったが、当時3歳の一人娘を連れて行くには忍びないとして宍道の回船問屋小豆屋に預けることとした「[http://www.gallaly-c.jp/?ID=14151 豊龍寺　ギャラリーＣ 松江市宍道ふれあい交流館（島根県松江市宍道町）]」松江市宍道ふれあい交流館ホームページ。その際、金襴の打ちかけとともに娘に持たせたのが本太刀であるとされ、現在も同家に伝わっている。なお松江市によれば、本太刀の作者は本記事で記載される三条宗近ではなく、[[室町時代]]の作ではないかしている「[http://www1.city.matsue.shimane.jp/bunka/bunkazai/siteibun/sisitei.html 松江市:暮らしのガイド:市指定文化財]」松江市ホームページ。松江市指定文化財（[[1989年]]（平成元年）[[7月1日]]指定）、なお美術工芸品ではなく歴史資料として指定されている。 ;八多神社所有の脇差 :[[脇差]]「[http://www.furemachihata.jp/publics/index/15/ 八多町の伝統文化・名所旧跡]」八多ふれあいのまちづくり協議会ホームページ。[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]][[北区 (神戸市)|北区]][[八多神社]]所有。社伝では、同社は宗近が崇拝していた神社であったとしており、宗近から「日本一」と号する日本刀が奉納されたと伝わる八多村編著『[{{NDLDC|1150370/}} 八多村史]』、1933年。また[[摂津名所図会]]では同地周辺に宗近の旧屋があったと記載されており、宗近と縁があるとされる神社である。現在、同社には、銘に宗近の名が切られた脇差、白鞘に宗近が奉納されたと書かれた脇差、宗近が奉納したとされる脇差が保管されている。なお、いずれも真偽は定かではない。 ;三条小鍛冶宗近作太刀 :太刀。「太刀　三条小鍛冶宗近作　二尺一寸　一腰　蓮祖の所持諸弘通の節之レを帯す、北条弥源太殿より之レを献ず」（富士大石寺明細誌）日蓮聖人所持。 ;三条小鍛冶宗近作御太刀 :太刀。(公財)[[占出山]]保存会所有。[[祇園祭]]の[[山鉾]]の一つ、[[占出山]]の御神体、[[神功皇后]]が佩く太刀。太刀について、占出山町文書占出山町文書とは祇園祭の山鉾町の一つ，占出山町の文書で桃山時代～江戸時代のもの。中でも文化文政年間(1804～30)を中心とする町代改義一件についての文書が全体の2分の1を占める。京都市登録文化財。によると、[[天保]]五年([[1834年]])、京都の洛北大原郷内野村に[[嵯峨天皇]]の劔を打った真守の作と伝わる古刀を持っていた山内玄達という人物が鍛刀旧跡近くの飯道大権現に古刀を奉納すべく研磨を依頼したところ、三条宗近の銘がでてきた。そこで、宗近作と名高い長刀鉾にちなみ、占出山の神功皇后宮に山内法橋玄達が奉納すると書かれている。[[平成30年]]の祇園祭山鉾連合会の調査報告書によると、「太刀は、京都の古刀の特色を有しており、刀に漢字2文字の銘を認め、見ようによっては「宗近」とも読める。また、件の奉納経緯の譚の信憑性は高い。しかし、もっとも宗近の在銘の基準作はほとんど皆無に近く、これを宗近の真作と認めるのは容易でない。」とされ、この太刀が三条宗近作の太刀であるかは分かっていない。なお、宵山で展示されている太刀は、山鉾巡行用の複製品(国の[[重要有形民俗文化財]])で、実物は[[京都国立博物館]]に寄託されている。 ;穴守稲荷神社所有の太刀 :太刀。[[東京都]][[大田区]][[穴守稲荷神社]]が所有していたもの。[[1903年]](明治36年)に、御神宝として[[五辻家|五辻子爵家]]伝来の二尺三寸五分の太刀が奉納された。同年5月28日には、200を越える諸講社の講員等からなる大行列を成し、御宝剣遷座式が盛大に執り行われた{{Cite book|和書 |title=穴守稲荷神社縁起 |date=1912 |publisher=穴守稲荷神社社務所 |page=84 |author=金子胤徳}}。[[1945年]]([[昭和]]20年)[[4月3日]]から4日に行われた[[日本本土空襲|空襲]]で焼失した。 ==出典・注釈== == 参考文献 == *『名物刀剣 - 宝物の日本刀 -』（展覧会図録）、根津美術館・富山県水墨美術館・佐野美術館・徳川美術館、2011 ==関連項目== *[[日本刀一覧]] {{DEFAULTSORT:むねちか}} [[Category:日本刀の刀工]] {{People-stub}}

He lived in Sanjō, withi..."
revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ...

Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Original pages]] {{Isonokami Faith}}

[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Original pages]][[it:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Original pages]] {{Isonokami Faith}}

The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[Category:dd]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Isonokami Faith}}

Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Isonokami Faith}}

revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping
