[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == Related Topics == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Unsorted revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qqqqqqqqqq]] [[Category:qq deprecated start]][[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq deprecated close]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to qqth century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 20qq, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Unsorted [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to qqth century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 20qq, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Unsorted completion revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] to [[Category:completion tier 3]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to qqth century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 20qq, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:completion [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to qqth century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 20qq, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:Kk]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:completion He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to qqth century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 20qq, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:completion [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:completion [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Category:11]] [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." [[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] [[Category:Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:completion tier 3]][[Category: tier 3]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[Category:Category:11]] Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]]

[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]]
