revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Category:11]] [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:New]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]]

[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:New]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]]

revision.comment: 81 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:三条宗近 up to rev 96900356
revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|『月耕随筆 稲荷山小鍛治』[[尾形月耕]]。三条宗近が稲荷明神の化身とともに作刀する[[謡曲]]「[[小鍛冶]]」を題材としたもの]] '''三条 宗近'''（さんじょう むねちか）は、[[平安時代]]の[[刀工]]。[[山城国]][[平安京|京]]の三条に住んでいたことから、「三条宗近」の呼称がある。 古来、[[一条天皇]]の治世、永延頃（10世紀末頃）の刀工と伝える。観智院本[[銘尽]]には、「一条院御宇」の項に、「宗近 三条のこかちといふ、後とはのゐんの御つるきうきまるといふ太刀を作、少納言しんせいのこきつねおなし作也（三条の小鍛冶と言う。後鳥羽院の御剣うきまると云う太刀を作り、少納言信西の小狐同じ作なり）」とある「銘尽」の写本の画像は[http://www.ndl.go.jp/exhibit/50/html/wa1-4/mokuji.html 国会図書館サイト]を参照。当該記述は第35丁表にある。。日本刀が直刀から反りのある彎刀に変化した時期の代表的名工として知られている。[[一条天皇]]の宝刀「[[小狐丸]]」を鍛えたことが[[謡曲]]「[[小鍛冶]]」に取り上げられているが、作刀にこのころの年紀のあるものは皆無であり、その他の確証もなく、ほとんど伝説的に扱われている。実年代については、資料によって「10 - 11世紀」『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』45（東京国立博物館5 工芸）、朝日新聞社、1998、p.1 - 140（解説は小笠原信夫）、「12世紀」『名物刀剣 - 宝物の日本刀 -』（展覧会図録）、根津美術館・富山県水墨美術館・佐野美術館・徳川美術館、2011、p.32（解説は[[渡邉妙子]]）等と幅がある。 現存する有銘の作刀は極めて少なく「宗近銘」と「三条銘」とがある。代表作は、「[[天下五剣]]」の一つに数えられる、[[徳川将軍家]]伝来の[[国宝]]「[[三日月宗近]]」。 作風は板目肌がよく約み、地沸がつき、小乱れ刃、匂が深く小沸がついて、三日月形の「打のけ」と称される刃文などが見られる。 ==作刀== 三条宗近作とされる主な日本刀（実在が確認されていないものや本物が確実視されていないものも含む。）は以下のとおり。[[日本国]]やその[[地方公共団体]]が指定する文化財については、指定名称で記載した。 ;太刀銘三条（名物[[三日月宗近]]）  :[[国宝]]、[[徳川宗家]]伝来品、[[東京国立博物館]]所有。詳細は[[三日月宗近|当該記事]]を参照。 ;太刀銘三条 [[File:Tachi_sword_signed_Sanjo.jpg|thumb|太刀 銘三條]] :[[岐阜県]][[不破郡]][[垂井町]][[南宮大社]]所有。鎬造、庵棟、小切先、反り高く踏ん張りがある[[東京帝室博物館]]編『[{{NDLDC|1263478}} 東京帝室博物館復興開館陳列目録第6]』 東京帝室博物館出版、[[1938年]]。地鉄は小板目肌が約（つ）まり、地沸（じにえ）一面に付き、映り立つ。刃文は細微な沸出来の小乱れ、その間にやや長い尖刃現れ、足繁く入る、表裏に棒樋（ぼうひ）を掻き流し樋先上る。帽子は二重刃で匂口やや沈み、小丸にわずかに返っている。茎（なかご）は生ぶ、勝手下がりの鑢目を切り、佩裏（はきうら）の目釘穴の下に「三條」と銘を切る。長さ2尺5寸8分5厘、反り1寸5厘、元幅9分遊就館編著『[{{NDLDC|1139254}} 紀元二千六百年奉祝名宝日本刀展覧会出陳刀図譜]』遊就館出版、[[1940年]]。昭和初期に[[赤坂町 (岐阜県)|赤坂町]]の実業家である[[矢橋亮吉]]から同社に奉納されたものである「[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/kyoiku-bunka-sports/bunka-geijutsu/bunkazai-zuroku/kougeihin/tachi-sanjyo.html 岐阜県 : 太刀　銘　三条［たち　めい　さんじょう］]」岐阜県ホームページ。重要文化財（[[1934年]]（昭和9年）[[1月30日]]指定）昭和9年文部省告示第23号1934年に[[国宝保存法]]の国宝（いわゆる[[旧国宝]]）に指定され、[[文化財保護法]]の施行後は同法附則第3条第1項の規定により[[重要文化財]]の指定とみなされている。。同社は、毎年[[文化の日]]（[[11月3日]]）に一般公開している「[http://www.nangu-san.com/sub4/ 刀剣について]」南宮大社ホームページ。 ;太刀銘宗（以下一字不明）伝宗近[[File:Tachi_sword_signed_Mune_(rest_unclear)_attributed_to_Munechika.jpg|thumb|太刀 銘宗□ 伝宗近]] :[[福井県]][[小浜市]][[若狭彦神社]]所有。鎬造、庵棟、小切先、反りが高く、先反浅い。地鉄は極めて細美、板目肌で最も約（つ）まり、所々で大肌交じり、細かに沸（にえ）付く。刃文は小乱れ、匂深く小沸付き、表腰に梵字、裏鎬地に腰樋を刻む。茎は生ぶ、判然としないが勝手下がりの鑢目を切り、佩表に大振りで二字銘があり、上は「宗」、下は「近」と略判読されるが、半ばに目釘穴がかかり、朽ち込んでいる。長さ2尺6寸1厘、反り1寸6厘、元幅9分2厘。[[東京国立博物館]]寄託「[http://www1.city.obama.fukui.jp/obm/rekisi/sekai_isan/Japanese/data/066.htm 太刀 銘 宗□（伝宗近）１口]」小浜市ホームページ。[[1795年]]（寛政7年）[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]、[[小浜城 (若狭国)|小浜城]]代の[[酒井忠為]]が病気平癒の祈願の際に奉納したとされる。重要文化財（[[1912年]]（明治45年）[[2月8日]]指定）明治45年内務省告示第11号1912年に[[古社寺保存法]]の国宝の資格あるものと定められ、国宝保存法の施行後は同法附則第3項の規定により国宝（いわゆる旧国宝）として指定された物件とみなされ、文化財保護法の施行後は同法附則第3条第1項の規定により重要文化財の指定とみなされている。。 ;海老名宗近 :短刀。長さ1尺1寸2分本阿弥光忠編著『[[享保名物帳]]』[[1719年]]（[[享保]]4年）『享保名物帳』は後世付けられた書名。原本は伝わらず、写本は公儀（幕府）本系と本阿弥家の控帳を元にしたものとの2系統がある。。[[足利将軍家]]第8代[[足利義政]]所有として、正月の年中行事の儀礼の際に着用されていたと記録される『慈照院殿年中行事』（[[塙保己一]]編『[[続群書類従]]第23輯ノ下武家部』[[続群書類従完成会]]、[[1926年]]、第659武家部第5所収128ページ）。当時の刀装は、柄鞘あわせて1寸5・6分、赤銅の金具に、梨子地の鞘と柄、目貫には桐丸焼付、[[笄]]は銅製といった具合であった。その後[[豊臣秀吉]]に渡り、[[大坂の陣]]の際に消失したとされる。名の由来は、地名（現在の[[神奈川県]][[海老名市]]）又は人名からとされる。享保名物帳「古来之名物焼失の部」記載の名物である。[[徳川美術館]]には「宗近」銘の焼身の短刀があり、同館では「名物海老名小鍛冶」と称している「[http://www.tokugawa-art-museum.jp/planning/h24/01/image/list.pdf 【特別展】名物刀剣 展示作品リスト]」[[徳川美術館]]、2012年『名物刀剣 - 宝物の日本刀 -』（展覧会図録）所載の「名物帳記載刀剣の比較表」では、「海老名宗近」の現所蔵先を徳川美術館としている。。 ;鷹巣三条 :短刀、銘は「三条」。長さ1尺4寸。[[豊臣秀吉]]が所有し、後に[[島津氏]]に伝来したとされる。名の由来は、いずこかの山中にある大樹の[[鷹|タカ]]の[[巣]]で見つかったことからとされる。享保名物帳記載の名物である。別名、鷹巣宗近。 ;[[小狐丸]] :太刀。[[九条家]]伝来品だが、所在不明。詳細は[[小狐丸|当該記事]]を参照。 ;松代藩真田氏伝来の太刀 :太刀。鎺元までの長さ2尺5寸1分、重さ138匁。[[松代藩]][[真田氏]]所有の太刀として、江戸時代末期（1854年以降）の同氏所有の刀剣をとりまとめた『御腰物御元帳』（長野県立歴史館蔵『石坂家文書』中）に「三条小鍛治宗近御刀御白鞘」として記載されている北村典子「資料紹介「『御腰物元帳』と真田家伝来の御腰物」」、『松代』第18号、松代文化施設等管理事務所、2005年。[[真田宝物館]]に保管された折り紙その他の文書によれば、[[天明]]4年（[[1784年]]）正月に下氷鉋村（現・[[長野県]][[長野市]][[稲代町]]の一部）在住の杢左衛門が同藩主に献上したとされる北村典子「真田宝物館の刀剣類折紙」、『松代』第17号、松代文化施設等管理事務所、2004年。同年[[12月3日]]付け本阿弥光久による[[折り紙]]では金五百枚の代付がされている。{{要出典|date=2015年11月|刀身は奈良県の某家に所蔵されるが、今日的には銘の真偽は不明。銘は太刀裏に「宗近」。}} ;八代城主松井氏伝来の太刀 :太刀「[http://www.city.yatsushiro.kumamoto.jp/museum/event/2014/pdf/monolist.pdf もののふの美と心 ～八代城主･松井家の刀剣と刀装具～出品リスト]」[[八代市立博物館]]未来の森ミュージアム、2014年。一般財団法人松井文庫所有。無銘ではあるが、宗近作として[[熊本藩]][[八代城]]主[[松井氏]]から伝来している。附として[[17世紀]]に作成された黒漆鞘刀拵が現存する。 ;山城国宗近御太刀 :太刀。銘は「宗近」。[[皇位]]とともに伝わるべき由緒ある物（いわゆる御由緒物）であり、現在[[宮内庁]]侍従職が管理する[[皇室]]の私有財産（[[御物]]）である。[[小浜藩]][[酒井氏]]に伝来してきたものを、[[1909年]]（明治42年）に、第16代当主の[[酒井忠道 (伯爵)|酒井忠道]]が[[明治天皇]]へ献上したとされる。その後、皇室所有の刀剣は、[[第二次世界大戦]]後の[[財産税法|財産税]]や[[昭和天皇]]崩御の際の[[相続税]]として国庫に物納された国宝[[小竜景光]]（現・東京国立博物館蔵）、名物若狭[[正宗]]（現・宮内庁[[三の丸尚蔵館]]蔵）、名物京極正宗（同上）等が該当する。が、本太刀は[[小烏丸]]等と同様に引き続き御物として取り扱われている。御物の刀剣の多くは[[宮中祭祀]]などで役割を担っているが、本太刀も[[1月7日]]の[[先帝祭|昭和天皇祭]]で使用されることとなっている。 ;太刀　伝宗近 :[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[筥崎宮]]所有「[http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t57&fields=f2127&id=1307-81&bunrui=kenn_yuke_biju_koge 福岡県文化財データベース 太刀　伝宗近]」福岡県ホームページ。鎬造、庵棟、細身であり、地鉄は杢目肌に板目肌交り、刃文は直刃、表裏に棒樋を刻む。茎は生ぶで、目釘穴が2個ある。長さ約79.4cm、反り約3cm。同社では三条宗近の作と伝わっており、[[戦国大名]]の[[大内義隆]]が[[1543年]]（[[天文 (元号)|天文]]12年）に同社の本殿、拝殿等を再建した際に寄進されたことを告げる寄進状が共に保存されている。福岡県指定有形文化財（[[1956年]]（昭和31年）[[1月16日]]指定）。現在、同社の宝物館に保管されている。 ;小国神社所有の太刀2口 :太刀「[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/beliefs/ 御祭神とご由緒｜遠江國一宮 小國神社｜大己貴命（おおなむちのみこと／大国様）と小國神社の歴史]」及び「[http://okunijinja.or.jp/test/beliefs/heirloom/ 宝物｜御祭神とご由緒｜遠江國一宮 小國神社｜徳川家康公奉納の大刀や文化財の品々と遺跡] 」小国神社ホームページ。[[静岡県]][[周智郡]][[森町 (静岡県)|森町]][[小国神社]]所有。同社では、[[1572年]]（[[元亀]]3年）、[[武田信玄]]軍と[[織田信長]]・[[徳川家康]]の連合軍が争った[[三方ヶ原の戦い]]の際に、[[徳川家康]]が戦勝祈願として三条宗近の作とされる本太刀を同社に奉納したと伝わっている。 ;太刀銘伝三条小鍛治宗近作 :太刀「[https://www.city.niihama.lg.jp/soshiki/sportsbunka/shiteibunkazai.html 指定・登録文化財]」新居浜市ホームページ。[[愛媛県]][[新居浜市]][[一宮神社 (新居浜市)|一宮神社]]所有。本太刀は、同社の宝物として伝わっている「[http://ikkunomori.com/zinja/ 一宮神社 境内史蹟]」一宮の杜ミュージアムホームページ。新居浜市指定文化財（[[1965年]]（昭和40年）[[6月3日]]指定）。 ;三条宗近銘太刀 :太刀。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]、[[出雲国]][[意宇郡]]宍道（現在の[[島根県]][[松江市]]宍道町）周辺を本拠としていた[[武将]]の[[宍道政慶]]は、当時その一体を支配していた[[毛利氏]]の政策で[[長門国]][[阿武郡]]に転封となったが、当時3歳の一人娘を連れて行くには忍びないとして宍道の回船問屋小豆屋に預けることとした「[http://www.gallaly-c.jp/?ID=14151 豊龍寺　ギャラリーＣ 松江市宍道ふれあい交流館（島根県松江市宍道町）]」松江市宍道ふれあい交流館ホームページ。その際、金襴の打ちかけとともに娘に持たせたのが本太刀であるとされ、現在も同家に伝わっている。なお松江市によれば、本太刀の作者は本記事で記載される三条宗近ではなく、[[室町時代]]の作ではないかしている「[http://www1.city.matsue.shimane.jp/bunka/bunkazai/siteibun/sisitei.html 松江市:暮らしのガイド:市指定文化財]」松江市ホームページ。松江市指定文化財（[[1989年]]（平成元年）[[7月1日]]指定）、なお美術工芸品ではなく歴史資料として指定されている。 ;八多神社所有の脇差 :[[脇差]]「[http://www.furemachihata.jp/publics/index/15/ 八多町の伝統文化・名所旧跡]」八多ふれあいのまちづくり協議会ホームページ。[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]][[北区 (神戸市)|北区]][[八多神社]]所有。社伝では、同社は宗近が崇拝していた神社であったとしており、宗近から「日本一」と号する日本刀が奉納されたと伝わる八多村編著『[{{NDLDC|1150370/}} 八多村史]』、1933年。また[[摂津名所図会]]では同地周辺に宗近の旧屋があったと記載されており、宗近と縁があるとされる神社である。現在、同社には、銘に宗近の名が切られた脇差、白鞘に宗近が奉納されたと書かれた脇差、宗近が奉納したとされる脇差が保管されている。なお、いずれも真偽は定かではない。 ;三条小鍛冶宗近作太刀 :太刀。「太刀　三条小鍛冶宗近作　二尺一寸　一腰　蓮祖の所持諸弘通の節之レを帯す、北条弥源太殿より之レを献ず」（富士大石寺明細誌）日蓮聖人所持。 ;三条小鍛冶宗近作御太刀 :太刀。(公財)[[占出山]]保存会所有。[[祇園祭]]の[[山鉾]]の一つ、[[占出山]]の御神体、[[神功皇后]]が佩く太刀。太刀について、占出山町文書占出山町文書とは祇園祭の山鉾町の一つ，占出山町の文書で桃山時代～江戸時代のもの。中でも文化文政年間(1804～30)を中心とする町代改義一件についての文書が全体の2分の1を占める。京都市登録文化財。によると、[[天保]]五年([[1834年]])、京都の洛北大原郷内野村に[[嵯峨天皇]]の劔を打った真守の作と伝わる古刀を持っていた山内玄達という人物が鍛刀旧跡近くの飯道大権現に古刀を奉納すべく研磨を依頼したところ、三条宗近の銘がでてきた。そこで、宗近作と名高い長刀鉾にちなみ、占出山の神功皇后宮に山内法橋玄達が奉納すると書かれている。[[平成30年]]の祇園祭山鉾連合会の調査報告書によると、「太刀は、京都の古刀の特色を有しており、刀に漢字2文字の銘を認め、見ようによっては「宗近」とも読める。また、件の奉納経緯の譚の信憑性は高い。しかし、もっとも宗近の在銘の基準作はほとんど皆無に近く、これを宗近の真作と認めるのは容易でない。」とされ、この太刀が三条宗近作の太刀であるかは分かっていない。なお、宵山で展示されている太刀は、山鉾巡行用の複製品(国の[[重要有形民俗文化財]])で、実物は[[京都国立博物館]]に寄託されている。 ;穴守稲荷神社所有の太刀 :太刀。[[東京都]][[大田区]][[穴守稲荷神社]]が所有していたもの。[[1903年]](明治36年)に、御神宝として[[五辻家|五辻子爵家]]伝来の二尺三寸五分の太刀が奉納された。同年5月28日には、200を越える諸講社の講員等からなる大行列を成し、御宝剣遷座式が盛大に執り行われた{{Cite book|和書 |title=穴守稲荷神社縁起 |date=1912 |publisher=穴守稲荷神社社務所 |page=84 |author=金子胤徳}}。[[1945年]]([[昭和]]20年)[[4月3日]]から4日に行われた[[日本本土空襲|空襲]]で焼失した。 ==出典・注釈== == 参考文献 == *『名物刀剣 - 宝物の日本刀 -』（展覧会図録）、根津美術館・富山県水墨美術館・佐野美術館・徳川美術館、2011 ==関連項目== *[[日本刀一覧]] {{DEFAULTSORT:むねちか}} [[Category:日本刀の刀工]] {{People-stub}}

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[三条宗近]] to [[Sanjō Munechika]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import
revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|『月耕随筆 稲荷山小鍛治』[[尾形月耕]]。三条宗近が稲荷明神の化身とともに作刀する[[謡曲]]「[[小鍛冶]]」を題材としたもの]] '''三条 宗近'''（さんじょう むねちか）は、[[平安時代]]の[[刀工]]。[[山城国]][[平安京|京]]の三条に住んでいたことから、「三条宗近」の呼称がある。 古来、[[一条天皇]]の治世、永延頃（10世紀末頃）の刀工と伝える。観智院本[[銘尽]]には、「一条院御宇」の項に、「宗近 三条のこかちといふ、後とはのゐんの御つるきうきまるといふ太刀を作、少納言しんせいのこきつねおなし作也（三条の小鍛冶と言う。後鳥羽院の御剣うきまると云う太刀を作り、少納言信西の小狐同じ作なり）」とある「銘尽」の写本の画像は[http://www.ndl.go.jp/exhibit/50/html/wa1-4/mokuji.html 国会図書館サイト]を参照。当該記述は第35丁表にある。。日本刀が直刀から反りのある彎刀に変化した時期の代表的名工として知られている。[[一条天皇]]の宝刀「[[小狐丸]]」を鍛えたことが[[謡曲]]「[[小鍛冶]]」に取り上げられているが、作刀にこのころの年紀のあるものは皆無であり、その他の確証もなく、ほとんど伝説的に扱われている。実年代については、資料によって「10 - 11世紀」『週刊朝日百科 日本の国宝』45（東京国立博物館5 工芸）、朝日新聞社、1998、p.1 - 140（解説は小笠原信夫）、「12世紀」『名物刀剣 - 宝物の日本刀 -』（展覧会図録）、根津美術館・富山県水墨美術館・佐野美術館・徳川美術館、2011、p.32（解説は[[渡邉妙子]]）等と幅がある。 現存する有銘の作刀は極めて少なく「宗近銘」と「三条銘」とがある。代表作は、「[[天下五剣]]」の一つに数えられる、[[徳川将軍家]]伝来の[[国宝]]「[[三日月宗近]]」。 作風は板目肌がよく約み、地沸がつき、小乱れ刃、匂が深く小沸がついて、三日月形の「打のけ」と称される刃文などが見られる。 ==作刀== 三条宗近作とされる主な日本刀（実在が確認されていないものや本物が確実視されていないものも含む。）は以下のとおり。[[日本国]]やその[[地方公共団体]]が指定する文化財については、指定名称で記載した。 ;太刀銘三条（名物[[三日月宗近]]）  :[[国宝]]、[[徳川宗家]]伝来品、[[東京国立博物館]]所有。詳細は[[三日月宗近|当該記事]]を参照。 ;太刀銘三条 [[File:Tachi_sword_signed_Sanjo.jpg|thumb|太刀 銘三條]] :[[岐阜県]][[不破郡]][[垂井町]][[南宮大社]]所有。鎬造、庵棟、小切先、反り高く踏ん張りがある[[東京帝室博物館]]編『[{{NDLDC|1263478}} 東京帝室博物館復興開館陳列目録第6]』 東京帝室博物館出版、[[1938年]]。地鉄は小板目肌が約（つ）まり、地沸（じにえ）一面に付き、映り立つ。刃文は細微な沸出来の小乱れ、その間にやや長い尖刃現れ、足繁く入る、表裏に棒樋（ぼうひ）を掻き流し樋先上る。帽子は二重刃で匂口やや沈み、小丸にわずかに返っている。茎（なかご）は生ぶ、勝手下がりの鑢目を切り、佩裏（はきうら）の目釘穴の下に「三條」と銘を切る。長さ2尺5寸8分5厘、反り1寸5厘、元幅9分遊就館編著『[{{NDLDC|1139254}} 紀元二千六百年奉祝名宝日本刀展覧会出陳刀図譜]』遊就館出版、[[1940年]]。昭和初期に[[赤坂町 (岐阜県)|赤坂町]]の実業家である[[矢橋亮吉]]から同社に奉納されたものである「[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/kyoiku-bunka-sports/bunka-geijutsu/bunkazai-zuroku/kougeihin/tachi-sanjyo.html 岐阜県 : 太刀　銘　三条［たち　めい　さんじょう］]」岐阜県ホームページ。重要文化財（[[1934年]]（昭和9年）[[1月30日]]指定）昭和9年文部省告示第23号1934年に[[国宝保存法]]の国宝（いわゆる[[旧国宝]]）に指定され、[[文化財保護法]]の施行後は同法附則第3条第1項の規定により[[重要文化財]]の指定とみなされている。。同社は、毎年[[文化の日]]（[[11月3日]]）に一般公開している「[http://www.nangu-san.com/sub4/ 刀剣について]」南宮大社ホームページ。 ;太刀銘宗（以下一字不明）伝宗近[[File:Tachi_sword_signed_Mune_(rest_unclear)_attributed_to_Munechika.jpg|thumb|太刀 銘宗□ 伝宗近]] :[[福井県]][[小浜市]][[若狭彦神社]]所有。鎬造、庵棟、小切先、反りが高く、先反浅い。地鉄は極めて細美、板目肌で最も約（つ）まり、所々で大肌交じり、細かに沸（にえ）付く。刃文は小乱れ、匂深く小沸付き、表腰に梵字、裏鎬地に腰樋を刻む。茎は生ぶ、判然としないが勝手下がりの鑢目を切り、佩表に大振りで二字銘があり、上は「宗」、下は「近」と略判読されるが、半ばに目釘穴がかかり、朽ち込んでいる。長さ2尺6寸1厘、反り1寸6厘、元幅9分2厘。[[東京国立博物館]]寄託「[http://www1.city.obama.fukui.jp/obm/rekisi/sekai_isan/Japanese/data/066.htm 太刀 銘 宗□（伝宗近）１口]」小浜市ホームページ。[[1795年]]（寛政7年）[[11月1日 (旧暦)|11月1日]]、[[小浜城 (若狭国)|小浜城]]代の[[酒井忠為]]が病気平癒の祈願の際に奉納したとされる。重要文化財（[[1912年]]（明治45年）[[2月8日]]指定）明治45年内務省告示第11号1912年に[[古社寺保存法]]の国宝の資格あるものと定められ、国宝保存法の施行後は同法附則第3項の規定により国宝（いわゆる旧国宝）として指定された物件とみなされ、文化財保護法の施行後は同法附則第3条第1項の規定により重要文化財の指定とみなされている。。 ;海老名宗近 :短刀。長さ1尺1寸2分本阿弥光忠編著『[[享保名物帳]]』[[1719年]]（[[享保]]4年）『享保名物帳』は後世付けられた書名。原本は伝わらず、写本は公儀（幕府）本系と本阿弥家の控帳を元にしたものとの2系統がある。。[[足利将軍家]]第8代[[足利義政]]所有として、正月の年中行事の儀礼の際に着用されていたと記録される『慈照院殿年中行事』（[[塙保己一]]編『[[続群書類従]]第23輯ノ下武家部』[[続群書類従完成会]]、[[1926年]]、第659武家部第5所収128ページ）。当時の刀装は、柄鞘あわせて1寸5・6分、赤銅の金具に、梨子地の鞘と柄、目貫には桐丸焼付、[[笄]]は銅製といった具合であった。その後[[豊臣秀吉]]に渡り、[[大坂の陣]]の際に消失したとされる。名の由来は、地名（現在の[[神奈川県]][[海老名市]]）又は人名からとされる。享保名物帳「古来之名物焼失の部」記載の名物である。[[徳川美術館]]には「宗近」銘の焼身の短刀があり、同館では「名物海老名小鍛冶」と称している「[http://www.tokugawa-art-museum.jp/planning/h24/01/image/list.pdf 【特別展】名物刀剣 展示作品リスト]」[[徳川美術館]]、2012年『名物刀剣 - 宝物の日本刀 -』（展覧会図録）所載の「名物帳記載刀剣の比較表」では、「海老名宗近」の現所蔵先を徳川美術館としている。。 ;鷹巣三条 :短刀、銘は「三条」。長さ1尺4寸。[[豊臣秀吉]]が所有し、後に[[島津氏]]に伝来したとされる。名の由来は、いずこかの山中にある大樹の[[鷹|タカ]]の[[巣]]で見つかったことからとされる。享保名物帳記載の名物である。別名、鷹巣宗近。 ;[[小狐丸]] :太刀。[[九条家]]伝来品だが、所在不明。詳細は[[小狐丸|当該記事]]を参照。 ;松代藩真田氏伝来の太刀 :太刀。鎺元までの長さ2尺5寸1分、重さ138匁。[[松代藩]][[真田氏]]所有の太刀として、江戸時代末期（1854年以降）の同氏所有の刀剣をとりまとめた『御腰物御元帳』（長野県立歴史館蔵『石坂家文書』中）に「三条小鍛治宗近御刀御白鞘」として記載されている北村典子「資料紹介「『御腰物元帳』と真田家伝来の御腰物」」、『松代』第18号、松代文化施設等管理事務所、2005年。[[真田宝物館]]に保管された折り紙その他の文書によれば、[[天明]]4年（[[1784年]]）正月に下氷鉋村（現・[[長野県]][[長野市]][[稲代町]]の一部）在住の杢左衛門が同藩主に献上したとされる北村典子「真田宝物館の刀剣類折紙」、『松代』第17号、松代文化施設等管理事務所、2004年。同年[[12月3日]]付け本阿弥光久による[[折り紙]]では金五百枚の代付がされている。{{要出典|date=2015年11月|刀身は奈良県の某家に所蔵されるが、今日的には銘の真偽は不明。銘は太刀裏に「宗近」。}} ;八代城主松井氏伝来の太刀 :太刀「[http://www.city.yatsushiro.kumamoto.jp/museum/event/2014/pdf/monolist.pdf もののふの美と心 ～八代城主･松井家の刀剣と刀装具～出品リスト]」[[八代市立博物館]]未来の森ミュージアム、2014年。一般財団法人松井文庫所有。無銘ではあるが、宗近作として[[熊本藩]][[八代城]]主[[松井氏]]から伝来している。附として[[17世紀]]に作成された黒漆鞘刀拵が現存する。 ;山城国宗近御太刀 :太刀。銘は「宗近」。[[皇位]]とともに伝わるべき由緒ある物（いわゆる御由緒物）であり、現在[[宮内庁]]侍従職が管理する[[皇室]]の私有財産（[[御物]]）である。[[小浜藩]][[酒井氏]]に伝来してきたものを、[[1909年]]（明治42年）に、第16代当主の[[酒井忠道 (伯爵)|酒井忠道]]が[[明治天皇]]へ献上したとされる。その後、皇室所有の刀剣は、[[第二次世界大戦]]後の[[財産税法|財産税]]や[[昭和天皇]]崩御の際の[[相続税]]として国庫に物納された国宝[[小竜景光]]（現・東京国立博物館蔵）、名物若狭[[正宗]]（現・宮内庁[[三の丸尚蔵館]]蔵）、名物京極正宗（同上）等が該当する。が、本太刀は[[小烏丸]]等と同様に引き続き御物として取り扱われている。御物の刀剣の多くは[[宮中祭祀]]などで役割を担っているが、本太刀も[[1月7日]]の[[先帝祭|昭和天皇祭]]で使用されることとなっている。 ;太刀　伝宗近 :[[福岡県]][[福岡市]][[筥崎宮]]所有「[http://www.fsg.pref.fukuoka.jp/bunka/contents_detail.asp?table=t57&fields=f2127&id=1307-81&bunrui=kenn_yuke_biju_koge 福岡県文化財データベース 太刀　伝宗近]」福岡県ホームページ。鎬造、庵棟、細身であり、地鉄は杢目肌に板目肌交り、刃文は直刃、表裏に棒樋を刻む。茎は生ぶで、目釘穴が2個ある。長さ約79.4cm、反り約3cm。同社では三条宗近の作と伝わっており、[[戦国大名]]の[[大内義隆]]が[[1543年]]（[[天文 (元号)|天文]]12年）に同社の本殿、拝殿等を再建した際に寄進されたことを告げる寄進状が共に保存されている。福岡県指定有形文化財（[[1956年]]（昭和31年）[[1月16日]]指定）。現在、同社の宝物館に保管されている。 ;小国神社所有の太刀2口 :太刀「[http://www.okunijinja.or.jp/beliefs/ 御祭神とご由緒｜遠江國一宮 小國神社｜大己貴命（おおなむちのみこと／大国様）と小國神社の歴史]」及び「[http://okunijinja.or.jp/test/beliefs/heirloom/ 宝物｜御祭神とご由緒｜遠江國一宮 小國神社｜徳川家康公奉納の大刀や文化財の品々と遺跡] 」小国神社ホームページ。[[静岡県]][[周智郡]][[森町 (静岡県)|森町]][[小国神社]]所有。同社では、[[1572年]]（[[元亀]]3年）、[[武田信玄]]軍と[[織田信長]]・[[徳川家康]]の連合軍が争った[[三方ヶ原の戦い]]の際に、[[徳川家康]]が戦勝祈願として三条宗近の作とされる本太刀を同社に奉納したと伝わっている。 ;太刀銘伝三条小鍛治宗近作 :太刀「[https://www.city.niihama.lg.jp/soshiki/sportsbunka/shiteibunkazai.html 指定・登録文化財]」新居浜市ホームページ。[[愛媛県]][[新居浜市]][[一宮神社 (新居浜市)|一宮神社]]所有。本太刀は、同社の宝物として伝わっている「[http://ikkunomori.com/zinja/ 一宮神社 境内史蹟]」一宮の杜ミュージアムホームページ。新居浜市指定文化財（[[1965年]]（昭和40年）[[6月3日]]指定）。 ;三条宗近銘太刀 :太刀。[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]、[[出雲国]][[意宇郡]]宍道（現在の[[島根県]][[松江市]]宍道町）周辺を本拠としていた[[武将]]の[[宍道政慶]]は、当時その一体を支配していた[[毛利氏]]の政策で[[長門国]][[阿武郡]]に転封となったが、当時3歳の一人娘を連れて行くには忍びないとして宍道の回船問屋小豆屋に預けることとした「[http://www.gallaly-c.jp/?ID=14151 豊龍寺　ギャラリーＣ 松江市宍道ふれあい交流館（島根県松江市宍道町）]」松江市宍道ふれあい交流館ホームページ。その際、金襴の打ちかけとともに娘に持たせたのが本太刀であるとされ、現在も同家に伝わっている。なお松江市によれば、本太刀の作者は本記事で記載される三条宗近ではなく、[[室町時代]]の作ではないかしている「[http://www1.city.matsue.shimane.jp/bunka/bunkazai/siteibun/sisitei.html 松江市:暮らしのガイド:市指定文化財]」松江市ホームページ。松江市指定文化財（[[1989年]]（平成元年）[[7月1日]]指定）、なお美術工芸品ではなく歴史資料として指定されている。 ;八多神社所有の脇差 :[[脇差]]「[http://www.furemachihata.jp/publics/index/15/ 八多町の伝統文化・名所旧跡]」八多ふれあいのまちづくり協議会ホームページ。[[兵庫県]][[神戸市]][[北区 (神戸市)|北区]][[八多神社]]所有。社伝では、同社は宗近が崇拝していた神社であったとしており、宗近から「日本一」と号する日本刀が奉納されたと伝わる八多村編著『[{{NDLDC|1150370/}} 八多村史]』、1933年。また[[摂津名所図会]]では同地周辺に宗近の旧屋があったと記載されており、宗近と縁があるとされる神社である。現在、同社には、銘に宗近の名が切られた脇差、白鞘に宗近が奉納されたと書かれた脇差、宗近が奉納したとされる脇差が保管されている。なお、いずれも真偽は定かではない。 ;三条小鍛冶宗近作太刀 :太刀。「太刀　三条小鍛冶宗近作　二尺一寸　一腰　蓮祖の所持諸弘通の節之レを帯す、北条弥源太殿より之レを献ず」（富士大石寺明細誌）日蓮聖人所持。 ;三条小鍛冶宗近作御太刀 :太刀。(公財)[[占出山]]保存会所有。[[祇園祭]]の[[山鉾]]の一つ、[[占出山]]の御神体、[[神功皇后]]が佩く太刀。太刀について、占出山町文書占出山町文書とは祇園祭の山鉾町の一つ，占出山町の文書で桃山時代～江戸時代のもの。中でも文化文政年間(1804～30)を中心とする町代改義一件についての文書が全体の2分の1を占める。京都市登録文化財。によると、[[天保]]五年([[1834年]])、京都の洛北大原郷内野村に[[嵯峨天皇]]の劔を打った真守の作と伝わる古刀を持っていた山内玄達という人物が鍛刀旧跡近くの飯道大権現に古刀を奉納すべく研磨を依頼したところ、三条宗近の銘がでてきた。そこで、宗近作と名高い長刀鉾にちなみ、占出山の神功皇后宮に山内法橋玄達が奉納すると書かれている。[[平成30年]]の祇園祭山鉾連合会の調査報告書によると、「太刀は、京都の古刀の特色を有しており、刀に漢字2文字の銘を認め、見ようによっては「宗近」とも読める。また、件の奉納経緯の譚の信憑性は高い。しかし、もっとも宗近の在銘の基準作はほとんど皆無に近く、これを宗近の真作と認めるのは容易でない。」とされ、この太刀が三条宗近作の太刀であるかは分かっていない。なお、宵山で展示されている太刀は、山鉾巡行用の複製品(国の[[重要有形民俗文化財]])で、実物は[[京都国立博物館]]に寄託されている。 ;穴守稲荷神社所有の太刀 :太刀。[[東京都]][[大田区]][[穴守稲荷神社]]が所有していたもの。[[1903年]](明治36年)に、御神宝として[[五辻家|五辻子爵家]]伝来の二尺三寸五分の太刀が奉納された。同年5月28日には、200を越える諸講社の講員等からなる大行列を成し、御宝剣遷座式が盛大に執り行われた{{Cite book|和書 |title=穴守稲荷神社縁起 |date=1912 |publisher=穴守稲荷神社社務所 |page=84 |author=金子胤徳}}。[[1945年]]([[昭和]]20年)[[4月3日]]から4日に行われた[[日本本土空襲|空襲]]で焼失した。 ==出典・注釈== == 参考文献 == *『名物刀剣 - 宝物の日本刀 -』（展覧会図録）、根津美術館・富山県水墨美術館・佐野美術館・徳川美術館、2011 ==関連項目== *[[日本刀一覧]] {{DEFAULTSORT:むねちか}} [[Category:日本刀の刀工]] {{People-stub}}

[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]]

Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology\]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[en:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]]

The [[Noh]] play *[[Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[Inari Shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}}

The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}}

revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[National Treasure]] ''[[Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}}

The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}}

revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in [[Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}}

The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}}

revision.text: [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}}

See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:Sanjō Munechika]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[sn:anjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}}

revision.text: {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11355828]] Wikidata name: Sanjō Munechika [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}}
