revision.text: [[sn:commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11355828]] Wikidata name: Sanjō Munechika [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:qq]]

Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] [[commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11355828]] Wikidata name: Sanjō Munechika [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by [[:en:Ogata Gekkō|Ogata Gekkō]]. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play "[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] in [[:en:Yamashiro Province|Yamashiro Province]], which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Ichijō|Emperor Ichijō]], around the [[:en:Eien|Eien]] era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from [[:en:Chokutō|straight blades]] (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The [[:en:Noh|Noh]] play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]]* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :[[:en:Gifu Prefecture|Gifu Prefecture]], owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in [[:en:Tarui, Gifu|Tarui Town]]. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in [[:en:Obama, Fukui|Obama, Fukui]]. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the [[:en:Siege of Osaka|Siege of Osaka]]. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the [[:en:Tokugawa Art Museum|Tokugawa Art Museum]].Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the [[:en:Inari shrine|Inari shrine]] and prominently featured in the Noh play *[[:en:Kokaji|Kokaji]].* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

The {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji}}* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :{{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture}}, owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in {{ill|Tarui, Gifu|en|Tarui, Gifu|WD=Q1345531|lt=Tarui Town}}. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in {{ill|Obama, Fukui|en|Obama, Fukui|WD=Q41773|lt=Obama, Fukui}}. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the {{ill|Tokugawa Art Museum|en|Tokugawa Art Museum|WD=Q3077236|lt=Tokugawa Art Museum}}.Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} and prominently featured in the Noh play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji}}.* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text: [[co:commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] [[co:commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] [[sn:commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11355828]] Wikidata name: Sanjō Munechika [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by {{ill|Ogata Gekkō|en|Ogata Gekkō|WD=Q2574296|lt=Ogata Gekkō}}. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play "{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji}}."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province}}, which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}}, around the {{ill|Eien|en|Eien|WD=Q1324864|lt=Eien}} era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from {{ill|Chokutō|en|Chokutō|WD=Q63821|lt=straight blades}} (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji}}* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :{{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture}}, owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in {{ill|Tarui, Gifu|en|Tarui, Gifu|WD=Q1345531|lt=Tarui Town}}. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in {{ill|Obama, Fukui|en|Obama, Fukui|WD=Q41773|lt=Obama, Fukui}}. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the {{ill|Tokugawa Art Museum|en|Tokugawa Art Museum|WD=Q3077236|lt=Tokugawa Art Museum}}.Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} and prominently featured in the Noh play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji}}.* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

(*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from {{ill|Chokutō|en|Chokutō|WD=Q63821|lt=straight blades}} (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh}} play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji}}* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :{{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture}}, owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in {{ill|Tarui, Gifu|en|Tarui, Gifu|WD=Q1345531|lt=Tarui Town}}. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in {{ill|Obama, Fukui|en|Obama, Fukui|WD=Q41773|lt=Obama, Fukui}}. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka}}. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the {{ill|Tokugawa Art Museum|en|Tokugawa Art Museum|WD=Q3077236|lt=Tokugawa Art Museum}}.Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}} and prominently featured in the Noh play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji}}.* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}, which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}}, around the {{ill|Eien|en|Eien|WD=Q1324864|lt=Eien|1=Eien}} era (late 10th century). (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from {{ill|Chokutō|en|Chokutō|WD=Q63821|lt=straight blades|1=Chokutō}} (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :{{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}, owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in {{ill|Tarui, Gifu|en|Tarui, Gifu|WD=Q1345531|lt=Tarui Town|1=Tarui, Gifu}}. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in {{ill|Obama, Fukui|en|Obama, Fukui|WD=Q41773|lt=Obama, Fukui|1=Obama, Fukui}}. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka|1=Siege of Osaka}}. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the {{ill|Tokugawa Art Museum|en|Tokugawa Art Museum|WD=Q3077236|lt=Tokugawa Art Museum|1=Tokugawa Art Museum}}.Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}} and prominently featured in the Noh play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji|1=Kokaji}}.* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] [[co:commons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Sanjō Munechika]][[it:Munechika]][[ja:三条宗近]] [[File:Blacksmith Munechika, helped by a fox spirit, forging the blade Ko-Gitsune Maru, by Ogata Gekkō.jpg|thumb|*Gekkō Essays: Inari Mountain's Blacksmith*, by {{ill|Ogata Gekkō|en|Ogata Gekkō|WD=Q2574296|lt=Ogata Gekkō|1=Ogata Gekkō}}. Depicts [[Sanjō Munechika]] forging the blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru* with the aid of a manifestation of the Inari deity, based on the {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}} play "{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji|1=Kokaji}}."]] '''Sanjō Munechika''' (三条 宗近, ''Sanjō Munechika'') was a renowned [[:en:Heian Period|Heian Period]] [[:en:Swordsmith|Swordsmith]]. He lived in Sanjō, within the capital of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} in {{ill|Yamashiro Province|en|Yamashiro Province|WD=Q749276|lt=Yamashiro Province|1=Yamashiro Province}}, which earned him the name "Sanjō Munechika." Traditionally, Munechika is said to have been active during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}}, around the {{ill|Eien|en|Eien|WD=Q1324864|lt=Eien|1=Eien}} era (late 10th century). According to the *[[Kanchiin Hon Meizukushi]]* (*Records of Swordsmith Signatures*), under the entry for "the reign of Emperor Ichijō," it is stated: "Munechika of Sanjō, known as Kokaji (*Little Blacksmith*), forged the blade *Ukimaru* for the [[Retired Emperor Gotoba]], as well as the blade *Ko-Gitsune* for [[:en:Shinzei|Shinzei]], the minor counselor." (*Kokaji* refers to the Little Blacksmith.) This suggests his work marks the transition in [[Japanese sword]] history from {{ill|Chokutō|en|Chokutō|WD=Q63821|lt=straight blades|1=Chokutō}} (*chokutō*) to [[tachi|curved blades]] (*tachi*), earning him recognition as one of the greatest swordsmiths of the era. The {{ill|Noh|en|Noh|WD=Q226887|lt=Noh|1=Noh}} play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji|1=Kokaji}}* celebrates his forging of the sacred blade *Ko-Gitsune Maru*. However, no swords bearing his name with inscriptions from this period have been found, and much of his story is treated as legendary. The exact timeline of his life and work varies in sources, ranging from "10th to 11th century" ''Weekly Asahi Encyclopedia: Japanese National Treasures,'' vol. 45 (Tokyo National Museum: Crafts), Asahi Shimbun, 1998, p.1-140 (commentary by Nobuo Ogasawara). to "12th century" ''Famous Swords: Treasures of Japanese Blades,'' exhibition catalog, Nezu Museum, Toyama Ink Museum, Sano Museum, and Tokugawa Art Museum, 2011, p.32 (commentary by Taeko Watanabe).. Existing swords bearing the signatures "Munechika" and "Sanjō" are extremely rare. His most notable work is the [[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]] ''[[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]]'', one of the [[:en:Tenka Goken|Tenka Goken]] (*Five Swords under Heaven*), passed down through the [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] and currently preserved at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. The distinguishing features of his craftsmanship include a well-forged ''itame-hada'' (*wood-grain steel surface*), a ground covered in ''jine'' (*martensite particles*), a ''ko-midare hamon'' (*small irregular temper line*), and a crescent-shaped ''uchi no ke'' (*interior shadow*), all of which demonstrate a deep mastery of blade artistry. == Blades == Below is a list of notable swords attributed to Sanjō Munechika (including some whose existence or authenticity remains uncertain). For cultural properties designated by Japan or local authorities, the official name is used. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō" (*Mikazuki Munechika*)  :[[:en:National Treasure|National Treasure]], former [[Tokugawa shogunate|Tokugawa Shogunate]] heirloom, owned by the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. See the article on [[:en:Mikazuki Munechika|Mikazuki Munechika]] for details. ;Tachi Inscribed "Sanjō"  :{{ill|Gifu Prefecture|en|Gifu Prefecture|WD=Q131277|lt=Gifu Prefecture|1=Gifu Prefecture}}, owned by [[:en:Nangu Taisha|Nangu Taisha]] in {{ill|Tarui, Gifu|en|Tarui, Gifu|WD=Q1345531|lt=Tarui Town|1=Tarui, Gifu}}. The blade is a ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a high curve, small tip, and reinforced structure. Its surface features fine ''ko-itame'' grain and ''jine'' particles. The hamon is a fine ''ko-midare'' with some longer pointed sections. Classified as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.pref.gifu.lg.jp/culture/heritage/sanjyo-tachi.html Gifu Cultural Heritage Database] ;Tachi Inscribed "Mune- (last character unclear)"Attributed to Munechika :Owned by [[Wakasa Hiko Shrine]] in {{ill|Obama, Fukui|en|Obama, Fukui|WD=Q41773|lt=Obama, Fukui|1=Obama, Fukui}}. The blade is ''shinogi-zukuri'' with a slightly shallow tip curve and distinctive temper line. Preserved as an Important Cultural Property.[http://www.city.obama.fukui.jp/culture/sanjyo-munechika.html Obama Cultural Property Database] ;Tanto (*Ebina Munechika*) :A famous blade listed in the ''Kyōhō Meibutsu Chō'' but lost during the {{ill|Siege of Osaka|en|Siege of Osaka|WD=Q475780|lt=Siege of Osaka|1=Siege of Osaka}}. Currently, a scorched tanto inscribed "Munechika" is held at the {{ill|Tokugawa Art Museum|en|Tokugawa Art Museum|WD=Q3077236|lt=Tokugawa Art Museum|1=Tokugawa Art Museum}}.Tokugawa Art Museum Special Exhibit, 2012 ;[[Ko-Gitsune Maru]] :A legendary sword created for the {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}} and prominently featured in the Noh play *{{ill|Kokaji|en|Kokaji|WD=Q48989608|lt=Kokaji|1=Kokaji}}.* The actual blade’s whereabouts are unknown. ;Tachi :Preserved by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} and classified as an [[Imperial Property]]. Used ceremonially during the [[Shōwa Emperor Memorial Rites]]. == References == == See Also == *[[:en:List of Japanese swords|List of Japanese swords]] {{Isonokami Faith}} {{translated page|ja|三条宗近|version=96900356|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[it:Munechika]] [[ja:三条宗近]] [[it:Sanjō Munechika]] {{Commons category|Sanjō Munechika}} {{wikidata link|Q11355828}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Japanese swordsmiths]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
